notes.note: IMP. = Imperator, an honorific salutation acknowledging Caesar's military command; by 45 BC Caesar held this title as permanent commander-in-chief, making it a standard element of formal correspondence addressed to him.

notes.anchor: never did they persuade the heart within my breast
notes.note: Greek quotation (Iliad 9.345, spoken by Achilles): 'all' emon ou pote thumon eni stethessin epeithen.' Cicero uses Homeric epic to describe, with self-deprecating irony, his own decision not to join Caesar's campaign --- as though he, like Achilles, refused to be moved by appeals to glory. [Greek phrase noted: three Homeric and one Euripidean tag in this letter.]

notes.anchor: I hate the wise man who is not wise on his own behalf
notes.note: Euripides, fragment 905 Nauck: 'miso sophisten, hostis ouch hautoi sophos.' Cited as the 'true precepts of Euripides' against Homeric heroics; Cicero presents the elder Precilius as endorsing this pragmatic wisdom, even while also quoting the Homeric ideal of aien aristeuein ('always be foremost') --- a deliberate paradox flattering Caesar by showing Cicero has chosen philosophical retirement, not opposition.

notes.anchor: I have employed with you a novel manner of letter, that you might understand this is no ordinary recommendation
notes.note: Cicero explicitly flags his use of Greek literary quotation in a Latin recommendation letter as a novum genus ('novel manner'), acknowledging that embedding Homer and Euripides is unusual; it signals both the intimacy of his relationship with Caesar (who shared his Greek education) and the unusual closeness of his bond with the Precilii.
